item.title: Risk Research & Threat Insights | Koi
item.description: Deep research and expert insights on software supply chain risk, browser extensions, AI security, MCP threats, and enterprise risks.

item.title: How We Hacked Multi-Billion Dollar Companies Using a Fake VSCode Extension
item.description: How we hacked multi-billion dollar companies in 30 minutes: a fake VSCode extension exposes the urgent need for better extension security.

item.title: Exposing Malicious Extensions: Shocking Statistics from the VS Code Marketplace
item.description: Exposing malicious extensions: shocking statistics from the VS Code Marketplace reveal hidden threats and major security risks for developers.

item.title: Uncovering Design Flaws of Visual Studio Code Extensions
item.description: Uncovering design flaws in Visual Studio Code extensions, exposing security risks, architectural weaknesses, and real world impact

item.title: Introducing ExtensionTotal: How to Assess Risk in VS Code Extensions
item.description: Introducing ExtensionTotal, your complete solution for assessing risk in VS Code extensions, ensuring security, compliance, and productivity.

item.title: 4.3 Million Browsers Infected: Inside ShadyPanda's 7-Year Malware Campaign
item.description: ShadyPanda’s seven-year campaign infected 4.3 million browsers, spreading malware undetected and endangering user security worldwide.

item.title: Breaking the Internet: The Aftermath Of Our Research
item.description: Our research sent shockwaves through the web, discover the aftermath and new internet challenges revealed by our groundbreaking findings.

item.title: A Month Of Malware In The Chrome Web Store
item.description: A Month of Malware in the Chrome Web Store reveals ongoing threats as malicious extensions compromise user security and privacy at scale.

item.title: The Malicious VS Code Theme That Fooled Millions
item.description: A Wolf in Dark Mode: Millions were deceived by a malicious VS Code theme, turning a simple UI change into a hidden malware threat.

item.title: AgreeToSteal: The First Malicious Outlook Add-In Leads to 4,000 Stolen Credentials
item.description: A legitimate Outlook add-in was abandoned by its developer and silently taken over by a phishing operation. Over 4,000 credentials were stolen through Microsoft's own add-in store.

item.title: Amazon’s AI Assistant Almost Nuked A Million Developer’s Production Environments
item.description: Amazon’s AI assistant nearly destroyed a million developers’ production environments, revealing critical risks in automated cloud operations.

item.title: Brew Hijack: Serving Malware Over Homebrew’s Core Tap
item.description: Brew Hijack exposed 50,000 users by delivering malware through Homebrew’s Core Tap, highlighting risks in popular package managers.

item.title: ClawHavoc: 341 Malicious Clawed Skills Found by the Bot They Were Targeting
item.description: We audited all 2,632 skills on ClawHub and found 341 were malicious - 335 from a single campaign we're calling ClawHavoc. The attack exploits the trust between AI bots and their users, tricking them into installing infostealers disguised as legitimate tools.

item.title: Command Injection Flaw in Framelink Figma MCP Server Puts Nearly 1 Million Downloads at Risk
item.description: A command injection flaw in Framelink Figma MCP Server exposes nearly 1 million downloads to risk, enabling attackers to execute malicious code.

item.title: DarkSpectre: Unmasking the Threat Actor Behind 8.8 Million Infected Browsers
item.description: Unmasking DarkSpectre: Discover the threat actor behind 8.8 million infected browsers and how to protect your data.

item.title: Embracing a New Domain Name: koi.ai
item.description: Our shift from koi.security to koi.ai marks the next chapter in our journey to redefine endpoint security for the AI-powered era. Find more here.

item.title: FoxyWallet: 40+ Malicious Firefox Extensions Exposed
item.description: FoxyWallet exposes over 40 malicious Firefox extensions, revealing a widespread campaign targeting users with data theft and hidden malware.

item.title: First Self-Propagating Worm Using Invisible Code Hits OpenVSX Marketplace
item.description: First self-propagating worm with invisible code targets the OpenVSX Marketplace, silently spreading across developer environments undetected.

item.title: GlassWorm Goes Mac: Fresh Infrastructure, New Tricks
item.description: GlassWorm expands to Mac with new infrastructure and tricks, learn how this malware evolves and threatens Apple devices.

item.title: GlassWorm Goes Native: Same Infrastructure, Hardened Delivery
item.description: GlassWorm upgrades its malware tactics by delivering native payloads with hardened infrastructure, increasing risks for enterprises.

item.title: GlassWorm Returns: New Wave Strikes as We Expose Attacker Infrastructure
item.description: Koi Research reports a new wave of GlassWorm infections targeting OpenVSX/VS Code extensions, and details infrastructure and victim-impact findings from the attacker’s servers.

item.title: Google and Microsoft Trusted Them. 2.3 Million Users Installed Them. They Were Malware.
item.description: Google and Microsoft trusted them, but 2.3 million users unknowingly installed malware, exposing data and systems to sophisticated attacks.

item.title: GreedyBear: 650 Attack Tools, One Coordinated Campaign
item.description: GreedyBear’s campaign deployed 650 attack tools in a single coordinated operation, targeting global systems and exposing massive security risks.

item.title: How We Prevented Cursor, Windsurf & Google Antigravity from Recommending Malware
item.description: Discover how we blocked Cursor, Windsurf, and Google Antigravity from recommending malware with advanced security measures.

item.title: Inside GhostPoster: How a PNG Icon Infected 50,000 Firefox Users
item.description: Discover how GhostPoster used a malicious PNG icon to infect 50,000 Firefox users and the risks behind seemingly harmless downloads.

item.title: Koi Raises $48M to Reinvent Endpoint Security for the Modern Software Stack
item.description: Koi secures $48M to revolutionize endpoint security, delivering innovative protection for the modern software stack and enterprises.

item.title: Koi to join Palo Alto Networks: A Defining Moment
item.description: Give me 2-3 sentences for meta description for this blog post: Koi to join Palo Alto Networks: A Defining Moment It’s hard to put today into words. If the past year hasn’t been crazy enough here at Koi, today, I’m incredibly proud (and deeply humbled) to share that Koi has signed a definitive agreement to be acquired by Palo Alto Networks. This marks a defining moment for Koi, and for the future of endpoint security. Excited doesn't even begin to cover how we feel today. The shared vision that Koi and Palo Alto Networks have for the future of the endpoint couldn’t be more aligned. Because the endpoint is changing. Not incrementally. Structurally. We are watching the laptop transform from a place where users run applications… into a platform where autonomous AI agents operate as persistent, privileged actors. Agents with access to the filesystem. To the shell. To credentials. To the browser. To enterprise systems. At the same time, the software running on those endpoints is going non-binary: packages, extensions, plugins, agent skills, MCP servers, model artifacts. Installed without oversight. Updated silently. Governed by no one. Over the past year, we’ve been researching, building, and defining what it means to secure this new reality. And today, announcing our intent to join Palo Alto Networks will allow us to bring that vision to the world at global scale. For our customers, I want to be clear about one thing. Our vision and product are not changing. The world we have been building toward is exactly the world Palo Alto Networks believes in as well. That alignment is precisely why this partnership makes so much sense. What will change, is our ability to execute at global scale, integrate deeply across the security stack, and deliver even more value. We are incredibly excited to continue this journey with you, and following the close of the transaction, backed by the full power of Palo Alto Networks. If you want to understand exactly why this moment matters, not just for Koi, but for the entire industry, Hadar Oren, SVP Cortex wrote a deeper piece outlining the shift we’re seeing and the security layer required to meet it. Read it here. For now, I want to pause and say thank you. To the Koi team, for an extraordinary, intense, and unforgettable year. For building something truly category defining. For pushing boundaries. For obsessing over the details. And for managing to enjoy the ride while doing it. To our customers, thank you for believing in us early, for challenging us, and for shaping the product into what it is today. Your trust and partnership are what got us to this moment. To our investors, including Picture Capital, NFX, Battery Ventures, Team8, and Cerca Partners, thank you for backing the vision and standing with us as we built it. And to our board members, Rakesh Loonkar, Gigi Levy Weiss, Barak Schoster Goihman, and Ori Barzilay, thank you for the guidance, conviction, and support that helped us grow Koi into what it is today. To Palo Alto Networks, thank you for sharing our belief in where the endpoint is headed and for your excitement in building that future together. And to Itay and Idan, what a year it has been. This is not the end of our story. It is the beginning of a much bigger one. Koi and Palo Alto Networks will be setting out to reshape endpoint security for an agentic world. We are just getting started. Stay tuned. You can read more about this announcement in the official press release here.

item.title: Malicious VS Code AI Extensions Harvesting Code from 1.5M Devs
item.description: Two popular AI coding extensions with 1.5M installs secretly harvest your entire codebase and profile you. Both are still live in the marketplace.

item.title: How We Could’ve Taken Over Every Developer Using a VSCode Fork
item.description: Marketplace Takeover reveals how a VSCode fork could've compromised every developer, putting millions at risk of large-scale cyberattacks.

item.title: MCP Malware Wave Continues: A Remote Shell in Disguise
item.description: A remote shell disguised as legitimate software powers the ongoing MCP malware wave, posing stealthy risks to countless systems.
